speaker: interviewer
text: The date is February 4th, 1996. The survivor is Henry Wiener. The interviewer is Rivka Wakslak. The city is Long Beach, New York in the United States of America. The language is English. This is Henry Wiener. I am Rivka Wakslak. We will be doing this interview in English. Mr. Wiener, thank you so much for participating in the project. Can you please tell me your full name and birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ysroel Chaim Wiener.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- and could you tell me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 12, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob. My mother's name was Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother, named-- actually, the Jewish name was [? Moshe, ?] but we called him [? Manyek. ?] And I had a sister whose name was Emilia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me a little bit about your family? What town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the town of-- the Polish name was 'Yur-kov' or Jurkow, whatever you call it. But we moved into Chorzow right before I started the first grade. So actually I was brought up in the city of Chorzow, had about 150,000 population before the war. It was in the smallest state of Poland, called Upper Silesia. It was a coal mine center. It was the richest part of Poland, really. It still is a coal mine center. My father was a businessman, and I was the oldest one of three children. My brother was two years younger. My sister was eight years younger than me. And this-- this was our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your home? Describe-- where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- we lived in-- in Chorzow, we lived on the fourth floor. I remember it was 83 steps because we have to bring, every other day, my job was to bring coal upstairs with two pails of coal. So every second it was my turn, [INAUDIBLE] and then it was my brother's turn, and was my father's turn. So we counted the steps. I remember like today. Happened to be 83 steps, so that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? large ?]-- the apartment was a bedroom, and a dining room, and a hall, and the kitchen. It was an average--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bedrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: One bedroom. But in the dining room, people slept also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had other people staying with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- it was a custom in my family that always we had somebody from the small towns, and one from my mother's side, a relative, and one was from my father's side, relative. As soon as my father saw to it that they learned their trade, the next couple came in. So that's the way it was brought up by us. That's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Chorzow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chorzow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me, what was a typical day like in your family when you were a small boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember in summertime was something unusual. We-- my mother packed some sandwiches for us, and in the summertime, one time I remember we went sort of like an outing, but before work. We went-- was-- in Poland, it was named [NON-ENGLISH], means the sort of a-- sort of small mountain of-- [GERMAN]. It was a German name. We went to eat in the morning, and then we all proceeded. We went to school. My father went to business. My mother went home. It's-- this sticked in my mind long time. And that's-- I mean, this was unusual, but it, it happened. Normally, we went to school. We all walked to school, and my father went to business. And my mother was at home, and took care of all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- in the public school I went to-- actually, it was a Jewish public school, but it was a public school attended only by Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many children were in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] I really wouldn't tell you. I don't know exactly. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: But all, all the children-- all the children-- Jewish children attended the school. And if you were more affluent, after a while, you could attend private schools where you were actually mixed with non-Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many kids were in your class in second or third grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 30, must have been. Must have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there more than one class for a grade? More than one first grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, it was one, and we all had Hebrew language as a-- as a language, as a-- as a language, and German was compulsory also. In other words-- actually, in our school, comparing to American, we had two foreign languages. And this was a normal, normal thing, it was not something unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the school day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there seven years. And then I went to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did your day, every day-- how long-- what time of day did you come home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. At least till two o'clock, 8:00 to 2:00. And it was six days a week with Saturday off. And Sunday, went to school. It was not five day. It was a six day curriculum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish subjects taught besides Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, if we-- actually, in the afternoon, we went to [NON-ENGLISH] in the afternoon as a separate curriculum.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school building?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school building, the same building, by a different teacher. And that's-- that's the way. It was a norm in our hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depends by whose standard. [LAUGHS] By American standard, yes, yes. I mean, everybody was-- was religious to a certain degree. I mean, we didn't work on holidays and didn't ride on holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a-- can you describe how you celebrated Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover was celebrated-- I think it was-- I think it was everybody's favorite holiday [LAUGHS] because what I remember is we rearranged furniture all the time. For some reason, on Pesach, furniture was rearranged. And we all had a-- my father wore a kittel, and, and the whole family gathered around the table. And-- was a festive occasion all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other-- did you have other family members living in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't have. The rest of the family lived about at least 80 to 100 miles away from us. So we didn't see them that often, maybe once a year, or even less. We were the only one that were-- that were that far away from it. All the rest family lived closer together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends in town when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends? Yes. Yes. Yeah, we had a lot of friends. We belonged to Maccabi, where the-- Jewish sport organization. That's where all the youth met, over there. And then, we were-- our youth at our town was Zionist oriented, where we all belonged to somewhere. Whether you belonged to Betar, what I belonged to, or my brother belonged to Akiva, not the same what I did. So we all belonged wherever we wanted to belong in order to-- and our goal was, was Israel. I mean, that time, it was Palestine because you saw it written on the-- on the walls, graffiti, [NON-ENGLISH], what meant, Jews, go to-- go to Palestine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who wrote--

speaker: interviewee
text: --by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Poles wrote it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did the Poles write this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They were-- they were not Jew lovers. Let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. As a matter of fact, we had-- the meeting of our Maccabi was every Thursday until about 11 o'clock. It was a meeting, weekly meetings. So after the meetings, there was a Polish fascist organization was running after us, tried to beat us up all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Narodowe means-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] narod means-- how do you say it? I forgot my-- I didn't-- I forgot my Polish. It was a Polish fascist organization, really, really. And at-- towards the end, towards end of-- I mean towards the-- before the beginning of the war, a lot of Jewish businesses were boycotted by this organization, where they write, don't buy by Jews, right in front of the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews were in this town? Were there a lot of Jews in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that many comparing to the population, maybe 3,000. Normally, it was about I would say, nine to 10%, on the average, of the Polish population were Jewish. But in our hometown, it was much, much less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family feel that-- was there a feeling that you wanted to leave Poland? Did you feel like this was getting--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Especially youth because youth were better educated than, let's say, than our parents, and our expectation was why our parents larger than ours, yet we couldn't get in into those industries because we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew could not become an officer in the Polish army. But, but before the war, if you had high school education, graduated, you had to become an officer. This was a norm. So, they rather give you honorable discharge, and not to be-- go to the army in order not to have Jews in the army. This was-- it wasn't-- it was not official, but unofficial. It was-- because-- didn't happen to me, I was not old enough, but it happened to some of our friends who were older than us, and that-- this-- that's what's happening before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So not-- not being able to go into the army would hinder you professionally afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not quite, but I'm just giving you for instance, for instance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have aspirations that you felt were not going to be able to be realized because of this anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we were oriented more to go to, rather at that time, to Palestine because we-- at that time was Aliyah Bet was in progress. And a lot of my friends did go 1938. They left, and they are in Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I confronted my parents, my mother was steadfastly against it. She says, look, I have only three children, whatever we did, we stick together. I don't have children for export. I mean, her thinking was different. And then, I mean, at that time, you listened to your parents, you know? You didn't go against them. But a lot of my friends now Israel. They-- they are the ones who in Aliyah Bet, and they served in the British Brigade. They speak very good English, and they are alive. Did your parents think about leaving Poland at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but there was no, no opportunity, no-- you had to be very rich, really. As a matter of fact, when I finished high school, I worked one year in a company named [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a very big company who produced bicycles and also radios, one of the first that had the-- with, with big boxed radios. And they were so big that they had, on the World's Fair, 1939, a representative here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came, I confronted my boss. I said, look, I have some family here. Let me go there, and maybe I could be able to stay there. So when I went to apply for a passport, they said, no, no, no. You are in the age of-- of army. You are 17 now. Next year, [? you will go ?] to the army. We cannot let you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look, I'm only [? on business. ?] I'm coming back. He says, post a bond of $10,000. You could go. $10,000 bond was not in our reach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did-- when did the situation really begin to feel that things were not good? Did you-- before the war actually broke out, did you feel really-- did-- did you start to feel fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's put it this way. We were brought up this kind of situation in Poland. It was not fear, really, but it was rather a sense of hopelessness, rather, not fear. Fear is not really the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sense of hopelessness for young people-- just, what are you going to do? [INAUDIBLE] You know, it's not-- you're really forced to go to business only. That's the only area that was open to you. But we had different aspirations, some of us-- a lot of us. And this was the hopelessness of the situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why we wanted to go rather to Palestine for that very reason. If I have to work on the field, I'd rather work there, for my own. You know, we had-- we were very nationalistic oriented at that time. I mean, I'm talking in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the war broke out? How did that-- how did things happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We knew the war was coming. I mean, you smell it in the air. And a month before the war broke out, we sent my then 10-year-old sister to my aunt near Krakow over there with my, my, my father's sister. And by the week before, we sent my mother and my brother to her also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said, I as the oldest one and father as the father, we stood to the last minute to see what we could salvage. And we couldn't salvage really much. Everything-- the bank closed. Everything closed. You know, everything closed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just picked up two valise, and we took the train, and wanted to go towards my aunt. But we only had maybe two station. The train was bombed. And we had to walk. We have to walk about towards my aunt at least 80 miles-- 70, 80 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was you and your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and I. And we walked. And after a couple days, we came there. And my mother and my brother was not there. Nobody knows what's happened to them. This was in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came there, the Germans were surrounding this area. And my uncle, my aunt's-- my aunt's husband, was ready to go. And he had two sons, my cousins, who were my sister's age. So my father said, look, I'm dead from walking. I'm not going anywhere. If I have to die, I'd rather stay here, not on, on the field. He says, you, as a young guy-- he says, you want to go with uncle, go. So I had a bicycle, took it-- he gave me a bicycle, my uncle, and we both went. Where did we go? Towards the Russian border. And who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about his sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: His sons were too young. He was-- about 10 years old or something. So they-- they stayed home with my aunt. And my father was there, and my, my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the Russian border. Do you remember the exact date of this? When did this take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was before Rosh Hashanah-- this I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Rosh Hashanah, couple days before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. And we were surrounded by the Germans, so we couldn't go anywhere. So my uncle said, what's the sense staying here? We gonna stay here? [? Let's ?] go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back home, two bicycles. My uncle went first. And when I was approaching a certain area, Polish soldiers were still sort of on defensive. And they confiscated my bicycle. And I-- I couldn't get a hold of my uncle, because he was ahead of me. And they didn't let me do anything. So I didn't have a bicycle. Then I found a bicycle, and I lost my uncle. I didn't know what happened to him. And then eventually, I came home about a day before Rosh Hashanah, I remember. I came home, and my aunt says, what happened to my-- to my husband, to your uncle? I told her. So she said, no, no no. He must have got killed or something, and you don't want to tell me the truth. I said no, aunt. I said, that's the truth. Anyway, after Rosh Hashanah, my uncle came. She says, what happened? He says, he didn't-- he came almost at Rosh Hashanah. He didn't want to travel Rosh Hashanah. So he stood in a neighborhood town with a, let's say, heavy day of travelling away. And after Rosh Hashanah, he came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I still didn't have knowledge of my mother and my brother. So after a month or so, we got a message that their train was bombed also when they went. And they were re-routed. And they went back home where we lived before. And they stood there-- a lot of people stood there-- and they waited. And then there came a-- an order to all the male Jews, clear the area of Upper Silesia and go to Russian border. And under penalty of shooting, they have to cross the border to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother found out about it. So she rented sort of a truck, loaded whatever she could on it, and came to us. And it's December 19-- November or December 1939. She came, and we were together as a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of our friends, my friends, were in Russia during the war. So this came some good sort of so to speak, but they were forced to go to-- to Russia, and they remained there. Two of my uncles, my mother's two brothers, you know, happened to be the same way in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your family at this point when your mother joined?

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- at this point, my family was at my-- my aunt's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in where? In Kra--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Klaj. The name was K-L-A-J in Polish. K-L-A-J. In Polish, name was Klaj, it was written. It is-- was in a-- It was a-- in a village. But there was not enough room for all of us. So my mother-- my father had a brother in Stary Wisnicz where actually my grandfather used to live before he passed away. And he said, look, I can rent you a room-- you know, a nominal fee, rent you a room. You come there. So we went there to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family, to live there in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Stary Wisnicz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we lived by selling our clothing. My mother brought clothing. You, you, you sell your clothing. You get wheat, or you get flour, you know, to bake your own bread, all those things. And we went to-- to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the quarters where you lived in Stary Wisnicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe where you lived in Stary Wisnicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He had a farm. My uncle had a farm. He had a house, and he had also many children. We lived in one bedroom, and he rented sort of part of a dining room for us. We made a separation, sort of. And we lived there. And, and eventually, we made a living going to town-- was Wisnicz Nowy, where Sally comes from. And we were cutting wood, all three of us-- my father, my brother, and I-- to cut wood for, for inhabitants of Wisnicz Nowy. Let's say you cut wood in large pieces, then you cut into small pieces. And that's how we made a living at that time. And also, the Hasidic Jews-- let's say, the order was from-- from the German government to the Jewish community that they want to have, every day, x amount of people to work for them. So you rented yourself out for them who didn't want to go. They paid you, and you went instead. To me, it didn't matter, because I spoke the perfect German, and my brother-- my brother, also. And we rented out. And we-- you know, that's how we made a living, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. I was 18. My brother was 16. And that's how I met Sally over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you given to do when you worked for the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- all kinds. We were picking strawberries. We were cutting wood. We were-- some kind of other manual labor. At one time, because I spoke German, so he took a few people. And they got from Hungary [NON-ENGLISH], big bottles of wine. And we were pouring wine into small bottles. You know, one of those-- this type of thing. We were a bit drunk after that, just from smelling. [LAUGHS] But at that time-- but you were confined into the area. You're not allowed under penalty of-- everything was penalty of death. There was no, no fine for, for money-- everything was penalty of death. You cannot go out of the area unless you had a special pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: What area was that? In Wisnicz. Let's say in Wisnicz Nowy. Let's say from Stary Wisnicz to Nowy, you could go. But if you wanted to go to neighborhood town, different town, you couldn't go. You had to be confined, or you had to have a special pass. And did you-- were people-- did people incur the penalty? Did you see people being killed for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not there. Not then, there. You heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard that they-- that they caught some, some people, and this. And they, they shot them there. But in this-- at that time, in this town, no. They didn't kill at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was like-- life like at that time under the German occupation? What did they-- what kind of rules where there, besides staying in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rules-- all the rules are under penalty of death. There's one, one rule, penalty-- whatever they want to, penalty of death. If they caught you without the armband, Magen David, penalty of death. They caught you curfew after certain hour, penalty-- everything was penalty of death. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And people were killed for not having an arm band.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people not killed, but people wore it [INAUDIBLE] because only those who wanted really escape or, not being recognized as Jews didn't wear it. Otherwise--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, especially women. Women, you-- you couldn't prove that they're not Jewish. You know, male, you, you, you prove it. That's-- that's why-- very heavily dependent upon, upon, upon women at that time and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example--

speaker: interviewee
text: Children could get away with many a thing that-- I'm not talking children. I'm not talking about 10 to 11-year-old. I'm talking about six and seven year old, eight year old. There was no-- they were not children anymore. They, they knew exactly the score. They knew ifs-- the ifs or outs of-- of the-- of the situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what could you ask of a child to do that would help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes they, they smuggled certain things to certain areas where you couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, in ghetto itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily there. Later on in ghetto, you couldn't get out of ghetto. Child could go, didn't wear a pass, and they didn't bother him, you know? They only looked for adults.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now-- let's talk now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they didn't have, no. Now, you know, in this area, at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Things were fairly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Things were fairly, fairly quiet, if you abide-- abide by the law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family find that they were eating OK? There was enough food at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was never enough food, never enough food because, don't forget one thing. We were refugees in my family. All the others, they're still stay in their houses. They were not yet-- they were infringing on them because they, they couldn't do certain things. But they were not thrown out of the house. They were still in their houses. You know, we were refugees. And some of us also-- some were from city of Krakow, also were thrown out. Those-- they were made a ghetto at that time. Those who lived behind the ghetto had to move to the ghetto or some place else. So they came to the town of Wisnicz. They lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was also, you know, refugees at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- but now, when you were in your uncle's farm-- Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and you were staying there, did it help that you were with an uncle, or--? No, no, no, no. We had problems with him. We had problems with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: That created stress.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, stress, and-- he blocked our entrance to, to the room, you know, sort of a blackmail type thing. He was sort of a black sheep in the family anyway. So we didn't want to make no issues. So we walked through to the window, you know. [LAUGHS] That's the way it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pause here for a tape change.
